$$

parts.content: Find $\frac{d y}{d x}$.
parts.markscheme:
$$
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-2 y+0.4 x y}{x-0.5 x y}=\frac{y(-2+0.4 x)}{x(1-0.5 y)} \quad \text { (M1)A1A1. }
$$

Note: M1 for chain rule, A1 per simplified term. [3 marks]

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the system at the equilibrium point where both populations coexist.
parts.markscheme:
Equilibrium: $x(1-0.5 y)=0,-2 y+0.4 x y=0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x=5, y=2 \quad$ (M1). Jacobian: $\left(\begin{array}{cc}1-0.5 y & -0.5 x \\ 0.4 y & -2+0.4 x\end{array}\right)$. At (5, 2): $\left(\begin{array}{cc}0 & -2.5 \\ 0.8 & 0\end{array}\right)$ A1. Eigenvalues: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}-\lambda & -2.5 \\ 0.8 & -\lambda\end{array}\right|=\lambda^{2}+2=0 \Longrightarrow \lambda= \pm i \sqrt{2} \quad$ (M1)A1. Eigenvector for $\lambda=i \sqrt{2}$ :
$\binom{-2.5}{-i \sqrt{2}}$ (M1)A1. Note: M1 for equilibrium, A1 for Jacobian, M1 for determinant, A1 for eigenvalues, M1A1 for eigenvector. [6 marks]

parts.content: Write the general solution.
parts.markscheme:
$$
\binom{x}{y}=A\binom{\sqrt{2} \cos (\sqrt{2} t)}{-\sin (\sqrt{2} t)}+B\binom{\sqrt{2} \sin (\sqrt{2} t)}{\cos (\sqrt{2} t)} \quad \text { (M1)A1. }
$$

Note: M1 for form, A1 for vectors. [2 marks]

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix using the characteristic polynomial
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is to find the eigenvalues of the 2×2 coefficient matrix from a linear system of differential equations, which is done via the characteristic polynomial of the matrix.

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of the coefficient matrix.
parts.markscheme:
- Write the system in matrix form:
$$ \begin{pmatrix} \frac{dx}{dt} \\ \frac{dy}{dt} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set up the characteristic equation:
$$ \det \begin{pmatrix} 2-\lambda & 1 \\ 3 & 4-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0 $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand the determinant to find the characteristic polynomial:
$$ (2-\lambda)(4-\lambda) - 3 = \lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 5 = 0 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve the characteristic equation:
$$ (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 1) = 0 $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the eigenvalues:
$$ \lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = 5 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Show that the system can be written in matrix form as $\binom{\dot{x}}{\dot{y}}=A\binom{x}{y}$, and find the matrix $A$.
parts.markscheme: Matrix form: $\binom{\dot{x}}{\dot{y}}=\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1 & 2 \\ -3 & -1\end{array}\right)\binom{x}{y} \quad$ A1A1. Note: A1 for correct form, A1 for matrix $A$. [2 marks]

parts.content: Prove that the eigenvalues of $A$ satisfy $(1+\lambda)^{2}+5>0$.
parts.markscheme: Characteristic equation: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}-1-\lambda & 2 \\ -3 & -1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(-1-\lambda)^{2}-(2)(-3)=\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+ 1+6=\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+7=0 \quad$ (M1). Discriminant: $\Delta=4-28=-24$. For real eigenvalues, $\Delta \geq 0$, but $(1+\lambda)^{2}+5=\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+1+5=\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+6$. Since $\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+7=0 \Longrightarrow \lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+1=-6 \Longrightarrow(1+\lambda)^{2}=-1<0$, but adding 5 gives $(1+\lambda)^{2}+5=4>0 \quad$ (M1)A1A1. Note: M1 for determinant, M1 for discriminant analysis, A1 for correct inequality, A1 for conclusion. [4 marks]

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors.
parts.markscheme: Eigenvalues: $\lambda^{2}+2 \lambda+7=0 \Longrightarrow \lambda=\frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-24}}{2}=-1 \pm i \sqrt{6} \quad$ (M1)A1. For $\lambda=-1+i \sqrt{6}:\left(\begin{array}{cc}-i \sqrt{6} & 2 \\ -3 & -i \sqrt{6}\end{array}\right)\binom{x}{y}=0 \Longrightarrow -i \sqrt{6} x+2 y=0 \Longrightarrow y=\frac{i \sqrt{6}}{2} x$. Eigenvector: $\binom{2}{i \sqrt{6}}$ (M1)A1. Similarly for $\lambda=-1 - i \sqrt{6}:\binom{2}{-i \sqrt{6}}$ A1. Note: M1 for equation, A1 for eigenvalues, M1 for eigenvector setup, A1 per eigenvector. [5 marks]

parts.content: Determine the direction of the phase portrait trajectories (clockwise or anticlockwise) when crossing the positive $x$-axis, justifying your answer.
parts.markscheme:
On positive $x$-axis $(y=0): \frac{d x}{d t}=-x, \frac{d y}{d t}=-3 x$. If $x>0, \frac{d y}{d t}<0$, so trajectory moves downward (M1)A1. Since eigenvalues are $-1 \pm i \sqrt{6}$, spiral is clockwise (positive imaginary part) A1. Note: M1 for evaluating derivatives, A1 for direction, A1 for clockwise conclusion. [3 marks]

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding the eigenvectors corresponding to eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly requires finding eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalues of a 2×2 matrix.

parts.content: Determine the eigenvalues of the system.
parts.markscheme:
1. Write the system in matrix form:

$$ \begin{pmatrix} \frac{d x}{d t} \\ \frac{d y}{d t} \end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} $$
<marks>M1</marks>

2. Set up the characteristic equation for the matrix:

$$ \det\begin{pmatrix} 2-\lambda & 1 \\ 4 & 3-\lambda \end{pmatrix}=0 $$
<marks>M1</marks>

3. Calculate the determinant:

$$ (2-\lambda)(3-\lambda)-(1)(4)=\lambda^2-5\lambda+6-4=\lambda^2-5\lambda+2=0 $$
<marks>M1</marks>

4. Solve the characteristic equation:

$$ \lambda=\frac{5\pm\sqrt{25-8}}{2}=\frac{5\pm\sqrt{17}}{2} $$

The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1=\frac{5+\sqrt{17}}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks> and $\lambda_2=\frac{5-\sqrt{17}}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Find the corresponding eigenvectors.
parts.markscheme:
1. Substitute the eigenvalue $\lambda_1=\frac{5+\sqrt{17}}{2}$ into the equation $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v} = 0$:
$$ \begin{pmatrix} 2-\frac{5+\sqrt{17}}{2} & 1 \\ 4 & 3-\frac{5+\sqrt{17}}{2} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} $$
<marks>M1</marks>

2. Simplify the first row equation:
$$ \left(\frac{-1-\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)x + y = 0 \implies y = \frac{1+\sqrt{17}}{2}x $$
<marks>M1</marks>

3. Choose a value for $x$ (e.g., $x=2$) to find the eigenvector:
$$ \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1+\sqrt{17} \end{pmatrix} $$
<marks>A1</marks>

4. Repeat for $\lambda_2=\frac{5-\sqrt{17}}{2}$:
$$ \left(\frac{-1+\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)x + y = 0 \implies y = \frac{1-\sqrt{17}}{2}x $$
<marks>M1</marks>

5. Choose a value for $x$ (e.g., $x=2$):
$$ \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1-\sqrt{17} \end{pmatrix} $$
<marks>A1</marks>

Therefore, the eigenvectors are:
$$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1+\sqrt{17} \end{pmatrix} \text{ and } \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1-\sqrt{17} \end{pmatrix} $$
<marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Accept any non-zero scalar multiples of these vectors.</note>

parts.content: Classify the origin and describe the behaviour of solutions near it.
parts.markscheme:
1. The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = \frac{5 + \sqrt{17}}{2}$ and $\lambda_2 = \frac{5 - \sqrt{17}}{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>
2. Since both eigenvalues are real, distinct, and positive, the origin is an unstable node. <marks>A1</marks>
3. Solutions will move away from the origin as $t \rightarrow \infty$. <marks>A1</marks>

Trajectories will follow the direction of the eigenvectors, moving outward.
<marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Suggest a practical interpretation of this system in terms of population dynamics.
parts.markscheme:
1. Suppose $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ represent two interacting populations.
2. The equations suggest mutual growth: population $x$ grows based on its own size and is also positively affected by $y$, and similarly for $y$ with respect to $x$. <marks>A1</marks>
3. This models a cooperative interaction (mutualism/symbiosis) where each species supports the growth of the other, leading to rapid population increase. <marks>A1</marks>
4. The system suggests exponential growth as both populations positively impact each other without any limiting factor, potentially indicating an unsustainable growth model. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the species variables
steps.content: To interpret the system, we first define our variables. In this context, $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ represent the population sizes of two different species interacting within the same ecosystem at time $t$. We will analyze how the growth rate of each species is influenced by the coefficients of the given differential equations.

highlights.text: Suggest a practical interpretation of this system in terms of population dynamics.

steps.title: Analyze coefficients for species $x$
steps.content:
Let's look at the first equation:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 2x + y$$

Using **Coefficient-Based Interaction Analysis**, we observe the signs of the coefficients:
*   The coefficient of $x$ is $+2$. Since it is positive, species $x$ grows based on its own size (intrinsic growth).
*   The coefficient of $y$ is $+1$. Since it is positive, the presence of species $y$ increases the growth rate of species $x$.

steps.title: Analyze coefficients for species $y$
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the second equation:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = 4x + 3y$$

*   The coefficient of $y$ is $+3$. This indicates that species $y$ also has positive intrinsic growth.
*   The coefficient of $x$ is $+4$. Since this is positive, the presence of species $x$ increases the growth rate of species $y$.

steps.title: Identify the biological relationship
steps.content: Because both species have a positive effect on the growth rate of the other, this system models a **cooperative interaction**, also known as **mutualism** or **symbiosis**. In this ecosystem, each species provides a benefit that supports the other's survival and reproduction.

steps.title: Evaluate long-term sustainability
steps.content:
Mathematically, since all terms are positive and there are no negative terms (like $-x^2$ or $-y^2$) to represent competition or resource limits, both populations will experience **exponential growth**. 

This suggests an unsustainable model in the long run, as the populations will grow indefinitely without any limiting factors or carrying capacity being reached.

steps.title: Define the populations
steps.content: In a biological context, we treat the variables $x$ and $y$ as the population sizes of two different species at a given time $t$. The derivatives $\frac{dx}{dt}$ and $\frac{dy}{dt}$ represent the growth rates of these populations.

steps.title: Analyze interaction coefficients
steps.content:
Let's look at the interaction terms in the given system:

$$\frac{d x}{d t}=2 x+y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=4 x+3 y$$

Notice that the coefficients for $x$ and $y$ in both equations are positive. This means:
- Population $x$ grows based on its own size and is further boosted by the presence of species $y$.
- Population $y$ grows based on its own size and is further boosted by the presence of species $x$.

highlights.text: \frac{d x}{d t}=2 x+y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=4 x+3 y

steps.title: Identify the biological relationship
steps.content: Since both species have a positive impact on each other's growth rates, this models a **cooperative interaction**. In biology, this is known as **mutualism** or **symbiosis**. Each species provides a benefit that helps the other population expand faster than it would alone.

steps.title: Assess growth and sustainability
steps.content:
From a sustainability standpoint, we look for limiting factors. In the IB data booklet, the **Logistic function** includes a carrying capacity ($L$) to show growth leveling off:

$$f(x)=\frac{L}{1+Ce^{-kx}}$$

However, in our system of equations, there are no negative terms or constraints to slow the growth down. Because the populations keep reinforcing each other, the system predicts **exponential growth** that will continue indefinitely. This suggests the model is likely **unsustainable** in a real-world ecosystem where resources like food and space are eventually limited.

steps.title: Summarize practical interpretation
steps.content: The practical interpretation is that these two species live in a **mutualistic relationship** where they support each other's growth. Because the model lacks limiting factors, it describes a scenario of **unconstrained, rapid population increase**.

steps.title: Define the context
steps.content: In population dynamics, we let $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ represent the number of individuals in two different species. Since we are dealing with biological populations, we focus our **phase plane reasoning** on the first quadrant, where $x \geq 0$ and $y \geq 0$.

highlights.text: practical interpretation of this system in terms of population dynamics

steps.title: Analyze the direction field
steps.content:
Let's look at the signs of the derivatives in the first quadrant:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{dt} &= 2x + y \\ \frac{dy}{dt} &= 4x + 3y \end{aligned}$$

If both $x$ and $y$ are positive, then both $\frac{dx}{dt}$ and $\frac{dy}{dt}$ are strictly positive. This means that for any starting population in the first quadrant, the **phase portrait** will show trajectories moving away from the origin toward infinity. Each species' growth rate is increased by the presence of the other.

steps.title: Identify the interaction type
steps.content:
Because the coefficients for the interaction terms ($+y$ in the first equation and $+4x$ in the second) are positive, the presence of species $y$ helps species $x$ grow, and vice versa. 

This describes a **cooperative interaction**, also known as **mutualism** or **symbiosis**. In this model, the two species support each other's survival and expansion.

steps.title: Evaluate long-term sustainability
steps.content:
From a phase plane perspective, there is no "stable" non-zero equilibrium point or "carrying capacity" (limiting factor) visible in these linear equations. 

As time $t$ increases, both populations will undergo **exponential growth** indefinitely. Practically, this suggests the model is a simplified version of reality, as it doesn't account for limited resources like food or space that would eventually slow this growth down.

parts.content: Find the equilibrium point.
parts.markscheme: Set $\frac{d x}{d t}=0, \frac{d y}{d t}=0: x+2 y=10,2 x+y=15 \quad$ (M1). Solve: from $2(x+2y=10)$ gives $2x+4y=20$; subtract $2x+y=15$ to get $3y=5\Rightarrow y=\frac{5}{3}$, then $x=10-2\cdot\frac{5}{3}=\frac{20}{3}$ (A1A1). Note: M1 for equations, A1 per coordinate.

parts.content: Use the substitution $X=x-\frac{20}{3}, Y=y-\frac{5}{3}$ to transform the system into a homogeneous system, and find its eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme: Substitution: $X=x-\frac{20}{3}, Y=y-\frac{5}{3} \Longrightarrow \frac{d X}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}, \frac{d Y}{d t}=\frac{d y}{d t} \quad$ (M1). System: $\frac{d X}{d t}=(X+\frac{20}{3})+2(Y+\frac{5}{3})-10=X+2 Y, \frac{d Y}{d t}=2(X+\frac{20}{3})+(Y+\frac{5}{3})-15= 2 X+Y \quad$ A1A1. Matrix: $\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right)$. Eigenvalues: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}1-\lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(1-\lambda)^{2}- 4=\lambda^{2}-2 \lambda-3=0 \Longrightarrow \lambda=3,-1$ (M1)A1. Note: M1 for substitution, A1 per equation, M1 for determinant, A1 for eigenvalues.

parts.content: Find the general solution of the original system, given the eigenvalues and eigenvectors from Part 1.
parts.markscheme: Eigenvectors: For $\lambda=3:\left(\begin{array}{cc}-2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2\end{array}\right)\binom{p}{q}=0 \Longrightarrow p=q$. Eigenvector: $\binom{1}{1} \quad$ (M1)A1. For $\lambda=-1:\left(\begin{array}{ll}2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2\end{array}\right)\binom{p}{q}=0 \Longrightarrow p=-q$. Eigenvector: $\binom{1}{-1}$ (M1)A1. Solution: $\binom{X}{Y}=A e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}+B e^{-t}\binom{1}{-1}$. Original: $\binom{x}{y}=A e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}+B e^{-t}\binom{1}{-1}+\binom{\frac{20}{3}}{\frac{5}{3}} \quad$ A1A1. Note: M1A1 per eigenvector, A1 per solution term.

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=5, y(0)=3$, find the particular solution and determine the limit of $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme: At $t=0: x=A+B+\frac{20}{3}=5, y=A-B+\frac{5}{3}=3 \Longrightarrow A=\frac{5}{3}, B=-\frac{10}{3} \quad$ (M1)A1. Solution: $\binom{x}{y}=\frac{5}{3}e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}-\frac{10}{3}e^{-t}\binom{1}{-1}+\binom{\frac{20}{3}}{\frac{5}{3}} \quad$ A1. Limit: Dominant term $e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1} \Longrightarrow \frac{y}{x} \rightarrow 1 \quad$ A1. Note: M1 for equations, A1 for constants, A1 for limit.

questions.specification:
This question will investigate the solution to a coupled system of differential equations when there is only one eigenvalue.

Solve the coupled system of differential equations

$$
\dot{x} = 3x + y
$$

$$
\dot{y} = -x + y
$$

A general solution to the coupled system of differential equations is given by

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} + B\begin{pmatrix} t \\ -t + 1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}
$$

parts.content:
Show that the matrix

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
3 & 1 \\
-1 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

has a repeated eigenvalue (i.e., only one distinct eigenvalue). Find this eigenvalue and an associated eigenvector.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the characteristic polynomial: $\begin{vmatrix} 3 - \lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow (3 - \lambda)(1 - \lambda) + 1 = 0$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$: $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow (\lambda - 2)^2 = 0$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

- State the result: So only one solution $\lambda = 2$ <marks>AG A1</marks>

- Solve for the eigenvector: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow p + q = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the eigenvector: So an eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

7 marks total

parts.content:
Using your eigenvalue and eigenvector, verify that

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
x \\
y
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
1 \\
-1
\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}
$$

is a solution to the above system.
parts.markscheme:
- Verify matrix multiplication: $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = 2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute into system: $\begin{pmatrix} 3x + y \\ -x + y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} = 2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

- Differentiate the given solution: $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}2e^{2t}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Conclude that $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ is a solution. <marks>AG</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content:

- State the particular solution: $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 20\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} + 30\begin{pmatrix} t \\ -t + 1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Express the system in matrix form and find the eigenvalues of the matrix.
parts.markscheme: Matrix form: $\binom{\dot{x}}{\dot{y}}=\left(\begin{array}{cc}-3 & 1 \\ -2 & -1\end{array}\right)\binom{x}{y}$ A1. Eigenvalues: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}-3-\lambda & 1 \\ -2 & -1-\lambda\end{array}\right|= (-3-\lambda)(-1-\lambda)-(-2)(1)=\lambda^{2}+4 \lambda+5=0 \quad($ M1 $)$. Solve: $\lambda=\frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16-20}}{2}= -2 \pm i$ (A1)A1. Note: A1 for matrix, M1 for determinant, A1 per eigenvalue. [4 marks]

parts.content: Find the eigenvectors corresponding to each eigenvalue.
parts.markscheme:
For $\lambda=-2+i$ :

$$
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-1 - i & 1 \\
-2 & 1 + i
\end{array}\right)\binom{x}{y}=0 \quad \text { (М1). }
$$

$-(1+i) x + y=0 \Longrightarrow y=(1+i) x$. Eigenvector: $\binom{1}{1+i} \quad$ A1. For $\lambda=-2 - i$, eigenvector: $\binom{1}{1 - i}$ (M1)A1. Note: Award M1 per eigenvalue equation, A1 per eigenvector. [4 marks]

parts.content: Write the general solution of the system.
parts.markscheme:
General solution:

$$
\binom{x}{y}=e^{-2 t}\left[A\binom{\cos t}{\cos t - \sin t} + B\binom{\sin t}{\sin t + \cos t}\right] \quad \text { (M1)A1. }
$$

Note: M1 for form, A1 for correct vectors. [2 marks]

parts.content: Determine the stability of the equilibrium point at $(0,0)$, with justification.

Eigenvalues $\lambda=-2 \pm i$ have negative real part (-2) R1. Equilibrium is a stable spiral A1A1. Note: R1 for negative real part, A1 for spiral, A1 for stability. [3 marks]

questions.specification:
A biologist is studying the population dynamics of two species in a shared habitat. The change in population of species X and species Y over time can be modeled by the following system of differential equations:

$$\frac{dx}{dt}=5x-2y, \quad \frac{dy}{dt}=2x+3y.$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.17—Phase portrait > Question Type 3: Finding the general solution to linear coupled differential equations with any type of eigenvalues
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Finding the general solution to linear coupled differential equations with any type of eigenvalues
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the characteristic equation/eigenvalues of a linear coupled 2D differential equation system, then uses eigenvalues to determine stability and describe local trajectories (phase-portrait behavior) near the origin.

parts.content: Determine the characteristic equation of the system and hence find the eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
1. Write the system in matrix form:

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{dx}{dt} \\
\frac{dy}{dt}
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
5 & -2 \\
2 & 3
\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
x \\
y
\end{pmatrix}
$$
<marks>M1</marks>

2. Set up the characteristic equation:

$$
\det\begin{pmatrix}
5 - \lambda & -2 \\
2 & 3 - \lambda
\end{pmatrix} = 0
$$
<marks>M1</marks>

3. Calculate the determinant:

$$
(5 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (-2)(2) = \lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 19 = 0
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

4. Solve the characteristic equation:

$$
\lambda = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{64 - 76}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{-12}}{2}
$$
<marks>M1</marks>

5. State the final eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda = 4 \pm i\sqrt{3}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Determine if the origin is stable or unstable based on the eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
1. State the eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda = 4 \pm i\sqrt{3}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

2. Conclude stability:

Since the real part of both eigenvalues is positive ($\text{Re}(\lambda) = 4 > 0$), the origin is an unstable equilibrium point.
<marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Describe the shape of the trajectories near the origin based on the eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
1. State that the complex eigenvalues imply a spiral pattern.
<marks>A1</marks>

2. State that the positive real part implies the trajectories spiral outward (unstable).
<marks>A1</marks>

3. Determine the direction of rotation. For example, at $(1, 0)$, $\frac{dy}{dt} = 2(1) + 3(0) = 2 > 0$, indicating counter-clockwise rotation.
<marks>M1</marks>

4. State that the spiral is counter-clockwise.
<marks>A1</marks>

5. Sketch the trajectory showing an unstable outward spiral pattern.
<marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > AHL 1.15—Eigenvalues and eigenvectors > Question Type 2: Finding the eigenvectors corresponding to eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding the eigenvectors corresponding to eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) also requires finding an eigenvector associated with the eigenvalue.

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right)$ and an associated eigenvector for each eigenvalue.
parts.markscheme:
Characteristic equation:

$\left|\begin{array}{cc}2-\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)-1(-1)=(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)+1$

$=(\lambda^2-3\lambda+2)+1=\lambda^{2}- 3 \lambda+3=0$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

Discriminant:

$(-3)^2-4(1)(3)=9-12=-3$ so

$\lambda=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}=\frac{3}{2}\pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$ <marks>A1</marks>

For $\lambda_1=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$, solve $(\mathbf A-\lambda_1\mathbf I)\binom{p}{q}=\mathbf 0$:

$(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i)p+q=0 \Rightarrow q=-(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i)p$.

Take $p=2$ gives $q=-1+\sqrt{3}i$, so an eigenvector is $\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

For $\lambda_2=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$, an eigenvector is the conjugate $\binom{2}{-1-\sqrt{3}i}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that $\binom{x}{y}=\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i} e^{\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\right) t}$ is a solution to the system.
parts.markscheme:
Let $\mathbf v=\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i}$ and $\lambda=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$.

Compute $\mathbf A\mathbf v$:

$\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right)\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i}=\binom{4+(-1+\sqrt{3}i)}{-2+(-1+\sqrt{3}i)}=\binom{3+\sqrt{3}i}{-3+\sqrt{3}i}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Compute $\lambda \mathbf v$:

$\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\right)\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i}=\binom{3+\sqrt{3}i}{\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\right)(-1+\sqrt{3}i)}$

Second component:

$\left(\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\right)(-1+\sqrt{3}i)=-\frac{3}{2}+\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}i-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i-\frac{3}{2}= -3+\sqrt{3}i$.

So $\lambda\mathbf v=\binom{3+\sqrt{3}i}{-3+\sqrt{3}i}=\mathbf A\mathbf v$ <marks>A1</marks>

Then

$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\mathbf v e^{\lambda t}\right)=\lambda\mathbf v e^{\lambda t}=\mathbf A\mathbf v e^{\lambda t}=\mathbf A\left(\mathbf v e^{\lambda t}\right)$, so it satisfies the system. <marks>M1 A1</marks>

parts.content: Find a second independent real solution to the system.
parts.markscheme:
From Part 0, take complex eigenpair $\lambda=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$ with eigenvector $\mathbf v=\binom{2}{-1+\sqrt{3}i}$.
Write $\mathbf v=\mathbf a+i\mathbf b$ where $\mathbf a=\binom{2}{-1}$ and $\mathbf b=\binom{0}{\sqrt{3}}$, and write $\lambda=\alpha+i\beta$ where $\alpha=\frac{3}{2}$ and $\beta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. <marks>M1</marks>

Then two real solutions are

$\mathbf x_1(t)=e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf a\cos \beta t-\mathbf b\sin \beta t)$ and

$\mathbf x_2(t)=e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf a\sin \beta t+\mathbf b\cos \beta t)$. <marks>M1</marks>

A valid second independent real solution is

$\mathbf x_2(t)=e^{\frac{3}{2}t}\left(\binom{2}{-1}\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)+\binom{0}{\sqrt{3}}\cos\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)\right)$,

i.e.

$\binom{x}{y}=e^{\frac{3}{2}t}\binom{2\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}{-\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)+\sqrt{3}\cos\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}$. <marks>A1</marks>

Verification (method): since $\mathbf v e^{(\alpha+i\beta)t}$ is a complex solution, its imaginary part is also a solution, so $\mathbf x_2(t)$ satisfies the system. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=5, y(0)=0$, find the particular solution.
parts.markscheme:
Use the real fundamental solutions

$\mathbf x_1(t)=e^{\frac{3}{2}t}\binom{2\cos\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}{-\cos\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)-\sqrt{3}\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}$,

$\mathbf x_2(t)=e^{\frac{3}{2}t}\binom{2\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}{-\sin\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)+\sqrt{3}\cos\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}t\right)}$.

General solution: $\mathbf x(t)=C_1\mathbf x_1(t)+C_2\mathbf x_2(t)$. <marks>M1</marks>

At $t=0$:

$\mathbf x_1(0)=\binom{2}{-1}$ and $\mathbf x_2(0)=\binom{0}{\sqrt{3}}$.

So $\binom{5}{0}=C_1\binom{2}{-1}+C_2\binom{0}{\sqrt{3}}$ gives

$2C_1=5 \Rightarrow C_1=\frac{5}{2}$,

$-C_1+\sqrt{3}C_2=0 \Rightarrow C_2=\frac{5}{2\sqrt{3}}$. <marks>A1</marks>

Particular solution:

$\binom{x}{y}=\frac{5}{2}\mathbf x_1(t)+\frac{5}{2\sqrt{3}}\mathbf x_2(t)$. <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A model for the interaction between two species, prey ( $x$ ) and predator ( $y$ ), in a certain ecosystem is given by the system of coupled differential equations:

$$
\frac{d x}{d t}=2 x-0.3 x y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=-y+0.2 x y,
$$

where $x$ and $y$ represent population densities (in thousands per $\mathrm{km}^{2}$ ) and $t$ is time in years.
(a) Find $\frac{d y}{d x}$.
(b) Find the equilibrium points of the system.
(c) For the equilibrium point where both species coexist:
(i) Write the system in matrix form and find the eigenvalues of the associated matrix. [4]
(ii) Determine the stability of this equilibrium point, justifying your answer.
(d) Sketch the phase portrait for the system in the region $0 \leq x \leq 5,0 \leq y \leq 5$, showing:

- The equilibrium points.
- At least three trajectories, including one near each equilibrium point.
- The direction of trajectories using arrows.
[4]
(e) Given initial populations $x_{0}=2, y_{0}=1$, use Euler's method with a step size of 0.1 years to estimate the populations after 0.2 years. [4]

parts.content:
At $\left(5, \frac{20}{3}\right)$, linearize the system. Jacobian matrix:

$$
J=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2 x-0.3 x y) & \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2 x-0.3 x y) \\
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-y+0.2 x y) & \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-y+0.2 x y)
\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
2-0.3 y & -0.3 x \\
0.2 y & -1+0.2 x
\end{array}\right)
$$

At $\left(5, \frac{20}{3}\right): y=\frac{20}{3}, x=5$,

$$
J=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
2-0.3 \cdot \frac{20}{3} & -0.3 \cdot 5 \\
0.2 \cdot \frac{20}{3} & -1+0.2 \cdot 5
\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
0 & -1.5 \\
\frac{4}{3} & 0
\end{array}\right)
$$

Eigenvalues: $|J-\lambda I|=\left|\begin{array}{cc}-\lambda & -1.5 \\ \frac{4}{3} & -\lambda\end{array}\right|=\lambda^{2}+2=0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda= \pm i \sqrt{2} \quad$ (M1)A1.
Note: Award M1 for correct Jacobian, A1 for correct matrix at equilibrium, M1 for determinant setup, A1 for eigenvalues. [4 marks]

parts.content: Find the equilibrium point for this system.
parts.markscheme: - Set derivatives to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} = 0 &\Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} = 0 &\Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Solve for $x$ and $y$: $\Rightarrow x = 10, \, y = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Define the equilibrium conditions
steps.content:
In the context of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** (or steady state) occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, represented by $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

This concept is a key part of **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.17)** regarding coupled differential equations. To find this point, we start by setting:
$$\dot{x} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{y} = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the linear system
steps.content:
Using the equations provided in the question, we substitute zero for the derivatives to create a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} 1) \quad x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2) \quad 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This matches the first step in the markscheme to earn the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Solve using substitution
steps.content:
To solve this algebraically, we can isolate $x$ in the first equation and substitute it into the second:

From equation (1):
$$x = 50 - 2y$$

Now, substitute this expression for $x$ into equation (2):
$$\begin{aligned} 2(50 - 2y) + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 100 - 4y + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 60 - 3y &= 0 \\ 3y &= 60 \\ y &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, find $x$ by plugging $y = 20$ back into our expression for $x$:
$$x = 50 - 2(20) = 10$$

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: In an IB exam, you can use your Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to solve systems of linear equations quickly. This is a great way to double-check your algebraic work.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation/Solver menu. Select 'Simultaneous'. Set the number of unknowns to 2. Enter the coefficients: a1=1, b1=2, c1=50 and a2=2, b2=1, c2=40. Press 'Solve'.

desmos.instructions: Type the two equations into the input bars. The intersection of the two lines is the solution.

steps.title: State the equilibrium point
steps.content:
The equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is:
$$(10, 20)$$

This means that when there are 10 units of population $x$ and 20 units of population $y$, the system is in a state where neither population is growing or shrinking (**A1**).

steps.title: Define the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
An equilibrium point in a system of differential equations occurs when the rates of change for all variables are zero. This means the population sizes are not changing over time. 

To find this point, we must set the derivatives $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for the Solver
steps.content:
To use the **Simultaneous Equation Solver** on your GDC, the equations need to be in the standard form $ax + by = c$. 

We set our expressions equal to zero and move the constants to the right side:

1.  $x + 2y - 50 = 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y = 50$
2.  $2x + y - 40 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 40$

This gives us a system of two linear equations with two unknowns ($x$ and $y$). This technique is covered in **Topic 1.8: Use of technology to solve systems of linear equations**.

steps.title: Solve Using GDC
steps.content:
Now, we input the coefficients into the GDC to solve for $x$ and $y$. Our coefficients for the matrix or solver are:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 50 \\ 2 & 1 & 40 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: State the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
The calculator provides the solution for our system:

$$x = 10, \, y = 20$$

Therefore, the equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This means that if there are 10 of type $x$ and 20 of type $y$, the populations will remain constant over time.

steps.title: Define Equilibrium
steps.content:
In a system of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

We start by setting our two equations to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange First Equation
steps.content:
To use the **Graphical Intersection Method**, we need to write our equations in the form $y = f(x)$ so we can enter them into a graphing calculator.

Let's rearrange the first equilibrium equation:

$$\begin{aligned} x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2y &= 50 - x \\ y &= 25 - 0.5x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange Second Equation
steps.content:
Now, we do the same for the second equilibrium equation to isolate $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \\ y &= 40 - 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the Intersection
steps.content: Now that we have two functions, $y_1 = 25 - 0.5x$ and $y_2 = 40 - 2x$, we can plot them and find where they cross. This intersection point is the solution that satisfies both equations simultaneously.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = 25 - 0.5x.
3. Enter f2(x) = 40 - 2x.
4. Draw the graph and use the 'Intersection' tool (usually under Analyze Graph or G-Solv).

ti84.instructions:

2. Enter Y1 = 25 - 0.5X.
3. Enter Y2 = 40 - 2X.
4. Press [GRAPH]. (Adjust [WINDOW] if needed, e.g., Xmax=50, Ymax=50).
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'.
6. Press [ENTER] for the first curve, [ENTER] for the second curve, and [ENTER] to guess.

desmos.instructions:

2. Type y = 40 - 2x into the second expression box.
3. Click on the point where the two lines cross to see the coordinates.

Using the graphing tool, we find that the lines intersect at:

This means the equilibrium point for the coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This corresponds to the first mark for setting equations to zero and the second mark for the correct values.

parts.content: If initially $x = 100$ and $y = 50$, use Euler's method with a time increment of 0.1 to find an approximation for the values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$.
parts.markscheme: - Using Euler's method formulas: $$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Calculate values at $t=1$: $x(1) \approx 848, \, y(1) \approx 837$ (3 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  3 marks total

steps.title: Define Euler's Method Formulas
steps.content:
To approximate the values of $x$ and $y$ in a coupled system of differential equations, we use the iterative Euler's method formulas:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}_n \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}_n \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + h \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the given differential equations $\dot{x} = x + 2y - 50$ and $\dot{y} = 2x + y - 40$ with a step size of $h = 0.1$, we get:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify Initial Conditions
steps.content:
We start at $t = 0$ with the following initial populations:

- $x_0 = 100$
- $y_0 = 50$
- $h = 0.1$

Since we want to find the values at $t = 1$, we need to perform 10 iterations (since $10 \times 0.1 = 1$).

steps.title: Calculate First Iteration (t=0.1)
steps.content:
Now we substitute $x_0 = 100$ and $y_0 = 50$ into our formulas to find the values at $t = 0.1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 100 + 0.1(100 + 2(50) - 50) \\ &= 100 + 0.1(150) = 115 \\ y_1 &= 50 + 0.1(2(100) + 50 - 40) \\ &= 50 + 0.1(210) = 71 \end{aligned}$$

So at $t = 0.1$, our approximation is $(115, 71)$.

steps.title: Complete Iterations Using Technology
steps.content:
Continuing this manual iterative substitution for 10 steps (until $t = 1.0$) gives the following results:

- $t=0.3: x_3 = 160.69, y_3 = 129.95$
- $t=0.4: x_4 \approx 191.8, y_4 \approx 171.1$
- ...and so on.

After 10 steps, we find:
$$x(1) \approx 847.61...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.69...$$

Create a recursive formula for two variables. 
Set Initial conditions: u1=100, v1=50.
Set formulas: 
an+1 = an + 0.1*(an + 2*bn - 50)
bn+1 = bn + 0.1*(2*an + bn - 40)
Look for the 11th term (which represents n=10 iterations).

steps.title: State Final Rounded Answer
steps.content:
Finally, we round our results to 3 significant figures as required by standard IB conventions (unless otherwise specified).

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

steps.title: Establish the Euler Formulas
steps.content:
Euler's method for a coupled system uses the gradient (derivative) at the current point to estimate the value at the next point. Given the step size $h = 0.1$, the initial values, and the differential equations, we define the iterative process as follows:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

This is consistent with the general Euler formula for coupled systems: 

$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}(x_n, y_n)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}(x_n, y_n)$$

steps.title: Configure GDC Spreadsheet
steps.content:
To avoid manual calculation errors across 10 steps, we use the Spreadsheet application on the GDC. We will set up three columns for $t$, $x$, and $y$. 

1.  In **Row 1**, enter the initial conditions: $t=0, x=100, y=50$.
2.  In **Row 2**, enter the formulas to reference the cells above:
    *   For $t$: `= A1 + 0.1`
    *   For $x$: `= B1 + 0.1 * (B1 + 2 * C1 - 50)`
    *   For $y$: `= C1 + 0.1 * (2 * B1 + C1 - 40)`
3.  **Fill Down** these formulas until you reach $t = 1$. This requires 10 iterations (reaching Row 11).

gdc.instructions:
1. Open Spreadsheet. 
2. Col A: Start at 0, next cell `= a1 + 0.1`. 
3. Col B: Start at 100, next cell `= b1 + 0.1*(b1 + 2*c1 - 50)`. 
4. Col C: Start at 50, next cell `= c1 + 0.1*(2*b1 + c1 - 40)`. 
5. Use 'Fill' command to copy Row 2 down to Row 11.

ti84.instructions:
The TI-84 doesn't have a full spreadsheet, but you can use Lists. 
1. Go to [STAT] -> Edit. 
2. In L1 (for t), enter 0, 0.1, ..., 1. 
3. In L2 (for x) and L3 (for y), you must calculate row-by-row manually or use a small program. 
Iter 1: 100 + 0.1(100+2*50-50) = 115 and 50 + 0.1(2*100+50-40) = 71.
Repeat until t=1.

desmos.instructions:
Use a table. 
1. Add a table with columns t, x, y. 
2. Set initial values in row 1. 
3. Since Desmos doesn't support recursive tables easily, use a script or compute sequentially: x1 = 115, y1 = 71, etc.

calculator.description: Calculate Euler's method iterations for the coupled system.

steps.title: Identify Values at t = 1
steps.content:
After 10 iterations, we look at the row where $t = 1$ (usually row 11 in the spreadsheet). The calculator provides the following approximate values:

$$x(1) \approx 847.611...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.690...$$

Rounding these to 3 significant figures as required by IB standards, we get:

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

parts.content: Extend this method to conjecture the limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - By extending the table, conjecture that: $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Show how using the substitution $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20$ transforms the system of differential equations into $$\begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2X + Y \end{aligned}$$
parts.markscheme: - State derivatives relation: $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20 \Rightarrow \dot{X} = \dot{x}, \, \dot{Y} = \dot{y}$ <marks>R1</marks>  - Substitute into original equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= (X + 10) + 2(Y + 20) - 50 = X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2(X + 10) + (Y + 20) - 40 = 2X + Y \end{aligned} $$ <marks>M1A1AG</marks>  3 marks total

parts.content: Solve this system of equations by the eigenvalue method and hence find the general solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system.
parts.markscheme: - Find eigenvalues: $$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow (1 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1 \text{ or } 3 $$ <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - For $\lambda = -1$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = -p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$.  - For $\lambda = 3$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - General solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1A1</marks>  8 marks total

parts.content: Find the particular solution to the original system, given the initial conditions of part 2.
parts.markscheme: - Substitute $t=0, x=100, y=50$: $$ \begin{aligned} 100 &= A + B + 10 \\ 50 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 30, \, B = 60 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write particular solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 30 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + 60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Hence find the exact values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$, giving the answers to 4 significant figures.
parts.markscheme: - Calculate values: $x(1) = 1226, \, y(1) = 1214$ (4 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the general solution
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation (using the eigenvector method), we have derived the general solution for the population of the two coral species. These analytical expressions for $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ are:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 60e^{3t} + 30e^{-t} + 10 \\ y(t) &= 60e^{3t} - 30e^{-t} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute the time value
steps.content:
We need to find the populations at $t = 1$. Substitute $t = 1$ into our general equations for $x$ and $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} + 30e^{-(1)} + 10 \\ y(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} - 30e^{-(1)} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: exact values of x and y when t = 1

steps.title: Evaluate population values
steps.content:
Using your GDC, evaluate the exact expressions to find the population values. 

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^3 + 30e^{-1} + 10 \approx 1226.168599 \\ y(1) &= 60e^3 - 30e^{-1} + 20 \approx 1214.095832 \end{aligned}$$

2. 60 * exp(3) - 30 * exp(-1) + 20

ti84.instructions:

1. 60 * e^(3) + 30 * e^(-1) + 10
2. 60 * e^(3) - 30 * e^(-1) + 20

desmos.instructions:
In separate rows, type:

2. y = 60e^3 - 30e^-1 + 20

steps.title: Apply significant figures
steps.content:
The question asks for the answers to 4 significant figures. 

For $x$: $1226.168...$ rounds to $1226$.
For $y$: $1214.095...$ rounds to $1214$.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The populations after one decade are:

$$x(1) = 1226$$
$$y(1) = 1214$$

steps.title: Identify system and initial values
steps.content:
To find the values of $x$ and $y$ at $t = 1$, we first need to identify the coupled system of differential equations and the initial populations provided in the earlier parts of this investigation.

The system is:
$$\begin{cases} \dot{x} = x + 2y - 50 \\ \dot{y} = 2x + y - 40 \end{cases}$$

From the previous parts of the question, we use the initial conditions at $t=0$:
$$x(0) = 100, \quad y(0) = 50$$

steps.title: Input into GDC Solver
steps.content:
We will use the numerical differential equation solver on your graphing calculator. This tool calculates values step-by-step using methods like Euler's or Runge-Kutta, which is perfect for coupled systems that are difficult to solve by hand.

You need to set the graph mode to **Differential Equations** and enter the equations as functions of $x$, $y$, and $t$. Note that on many calculators, $x$ and $y$ are represented as $y_1$ and $y_2$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Graphing application and change the graph type to 'Differential Equation'.
2. Enter $y1' = y1 + 2y2 - 50$ with $y1(0) = 100$.
3. Enter $y2' = 2y1 + y2 - 40$ with $y2(0) = 50$.
4. Use the table feature ([Ctrl][T]) to see the values at $t=1$.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and select 'DIFF EQUATIONS'.
2. Press [Y=].
3. Set $Y_1' = Y_1 + 2Y_2 - 50$ and $Y_2' = 2Y_1 + Y_2 - 40$.
4. Set initial conditions: $Y_{1i} = 100$ and $Y_{2i} = 50$.
5. Press [2nd][GRAPH] (TABLE) or [2nd][TRACE] (CALC) to find the values at $t=1$.

desmos.instructions:
1. Use the system of ODEs syntax: $x'(t) = x + 2y - 50$ and $y'(t) = 2x + y - 40$.
2. Set the initial points $(x_0, y_0) = (100, 50)$.
3. Look at the table of values generated for $t=1$.

calculator.description: Solve the system using numerical methods at t=1.

steps.title: State final values
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver, we find the following results at $t=1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &\approx 1226.168... \\ y(1) &\approx 1214.095... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding these to 4 significant figures as requested, we get:

$$x(1) = 1226, \quad y(1) = 1214$$

parts.content: Use part 6 to find limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - Identify dominant term: Dominant term is $60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$  - State limit: So $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 2, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - State conclusion: The populations do not converge (unstable trajectory). <marks>R1</marks>  1 mark total

parts.content: If instead the initial conditions were given as $x = 20$ and $y = 10$, find the particular solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system, in this case.
parts.markscheme: - Solve for constants: $$ \begin{aligned} 20 &= A + B + 10 \\ 10 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 10, \, B = 0 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 10 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 10, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - Reason about convergence: As $e^{-t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, the populations converge. <marks>R1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the solution form
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation, the general solution for the populations $x$ and $y$ is expressed as a combination of a constant equilibrium value and exponential terms. The exponents are determined by the eigenvalues of the system, which are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = 3$.

Given the specific initial conditions in part 10, the solution only contains the decaying exponential term associated with the negative eigenvalue. The general form for the population at time $t$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 10 + A e^{-t} \\ y(t) &= 20 + B e^{-t} \end{aligned}$$

where 10 and 20 are the equilibrium populations.

steps.title: Evaluate the long-term limit
steps.content:
To determine if the populations converge, we evaluate the limit as time $t$ approaches infinity ($t \to \infty$). 

We know that for any negative exponent, the term approaches zero as the variable grows:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t$ becomes very large, the parts of the population that vary over time ($A e^{-t}$ and $B e^{-t}$) disappear.

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = e^(-x). Use the trace tool to observe that as x increases, f(x) approaches the x-axis (y = 0).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press [GRAPH]. Use [TRACE] and scroll to the right to see the Y-value decrease toward zero.

desmos.instructions: Type y = e^(-x) into the expression bar. Observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0 as x increases.

calculator.description: You can verify the behavior of the exponential decay function on your calculator to see it approaching zero.

steps.title: Conclude on convergence
steps.content:
Because the time-dependent terms vanish, the populations will eventually reach a steady state. Specifically, the populations converge to the equilibrium values:

$$\begin{aligned} x &\to 10 \\ y &\to 20 \end{aligned}$$

Since the populations approach fixed constant values, we conclude that **the populations do converge** to the equilibrium values.

steps.title: Identify eigenvalues and equilibrium
steps.content:
To determine the long-term behavior of the coral populations, we first look at the eigenvalues of the system. From the previous parts, we found the eigenvalues to be:

$$\lambda_1 = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \lambda_2 = -1$$

and the equilibrium point to be $(10, 20)$. In a coupled system, the solution is a combination of exponential terms related to these eigenvalues:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_1 e^{3t} \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

where $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ are the eigenvectors. This system is technically unstable because $\lambda_1 = 3 > 0$, which would normally cause the population to grow away from equilibrium.

steps.title: Analyze initial conditions
steps.content:
The question specifies we use the initial conditions from the previous part. In those conditions, the state of the system lies exactly on the line of the eigenvector associated with the negative eigenvalue ($\lambda_2 = -1$). 

Because the initial state is on this stable eigenvector, the coefficient for the unstable growth term is zero ($c_1 = 0$). This means the general solution simplifies to:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

steps.title: Evaluate the limit
steps.content:
Now we examine what happens as time $t$ increases. For any value of $c_2$, we look at the limit of the exponential term:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t \to \infty$, the term $c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$ approaches the zero vector. This means the displacement from the equilibrium point disappears over time.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = e^(-x) and use the trace function to see the value as x becomes large.

ti84.instructions: Go to Y= and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press TABLE (2nd + GRAPH) to see values as X increases.

desmos.instructions: Plot y = e^(-x) and observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0.

calculator.description: Observe how e^(-t) behaves as t increases.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since the only remaining part of the solution decays to zero, the populations $x$ and $y$ will move closer and closer to their equilibrium values of $10$ and $20$.

Therefore, the populations **converge** to the equilibrium values.

$$

parts.content: Find the equilibrium points.
parts.markscheme:
Set $\frac{d x}{d t}=0, \frac{d y}{d t}=0$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$
-2 x+x y=0, \quad x-y=0 \quad \text { (M1). }
$$

From second: 

$y=x$. 

Substitute into first: 

$-2 x+x \cdot x=0 \Longrightarrow x(x-2)=0 \Longrightarrow x=0,2$ <marks>A1</marks> 

Equilibrium points: $(0,0),(2,2)$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
For the equilibrium point at $(1,1)$:

Find the Jacobian matrix and its eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
Jacobian:

$J=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-2+y & x \\
1 & -1
\end{array}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

At $(1,1): 

$J=\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)$  <marks>A1</marks>

Eigenvalues: 

$\left|\begin{array}{cc}-1-\lambda & 1 \\ 1 & -1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(-1-\lambda)^{2}-1= \lambda^{2}+2 \lambda=0 \Longrightarrow \lambda=0,-2 \quad$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

parts.content:
For the equilibrium point at $(1,1)$:

Classify the equilibrium point, justifying your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Eigenvalues $\lambda=0,-2$. 

Zero eigenvalue indicates a degenerate case (line of equilibria or saddle) <marks>A1</marks>

negative eigenvalue suggests stability in one direction <marks>R1</marks>

Saddle point <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=2, y(0)=0.5$, find the general solution.
parts.markscheme:
Matrix:

$\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

Eigenvectors: 

For $\lambda=-2:\binom{1}{-1} \quad$ <marks>M1</marks>

For $\lambda=0$ : 

$\binom{1}{1}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

General solution:

$\binom{x}{y}=A\binom{1}{-1} e^{-2 t}+B\binom{1}{1} \quad$ 

<marks>A1</marks>

Using $x(0)=2, y(0)=0.5$ :

Solve $A+B=2,-A+B=0.5 \Longrightarrow A=0.75, B=1.25 \quad$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the general solution using the eigenvalue method.
parts.markscheme:
- State the matrix of the system: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Find the eigenvalues using $|A - \lambda I| = 0$: $(2-\lambda)^{2}-1=\lambda^{2}-4 \lambda+3= 0 \Rightarrow (\lambda-3)(\lambda-1)=0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the eigenvalues: $\lambda=1, 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- For $\lambda=1$, substitute into $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=0$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\binom{x}{y}=0 \Rightarrow x+y=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the eigenvector for $\lambda=1$: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \binom{1}{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- For $\lambda=3$, substitute into $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=0$: $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\binom{x}{y}=0 \Rightarrow -x+ y=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the eigenvector for $\lambda=3$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = \binom{1}{1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the general solution: $\binom{x}{y}=A e^{t}\binom{1}{-1}+ B e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

8 marks total

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=1, y(0)=0.5$, find the particular solution.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $t=0$ into the general solution: $x(0)=A+B=1$ and $y(0)=-A+B=0.5$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve the simultaneous equations: $2 B=1.5 \Rightarrow B= 0.75$ and $A=0.25$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the particular solution: $\binom{x}{y}=0.25 e^{t}\binom{1}{-1}+0.75 e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Determine the asymptotic behavior of the particular solution as $t \rightarrow \infty$, including the equation of the asymptote.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the dominant term as $t \rightarrow \infty$: $0.75 e^{3 t}\binom{1}{1}$ (since $e^{3t}$ grows faster than $e^t$) <marks>M1</marks>

- State the equation of the asymptote: $y=x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Describe the trajectory: Moves away from the origin (positive eigenvalues indicate an unstable node) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the equilibrium point.
parts.markscheme:
Set $\frac{dx}{dt}=0, \frac{dy}{dt}=0$: 
$x+2y=7$
$2x+y=8$ <marks>M1</marks>

Solving the simultaneous equations: 
$x=3$, $y=2$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

parts.content: Use the substitution $X=x-3, Y=y-2$ to transform the system into a homogeneous system, and find its eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution:
$X=x-3, Y=y-2 \Rightarrow \frac{dX}{dt}=\frac{dx}{dt}, \frac{dY}{dt}=\frac{dy}{dt}$ <marks>M1</marks>

System:
$\frac{dX}{dt}=(X+3)+2(Y+2)-7=X+2Y$
$\frac{dY}{dt}=2(X+3)+(Y+2)-8=2X+Y$ <marks>A1</marks>

Matrix form:
$\begin{pmatrix} \dot{X} \\ \dot{Y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Characteristic equation:
$\det\begin{pmatrix} 1-\lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 1-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (1-\lambda)^{2}- 4 = \lambda^{2}-2\lambda-3 = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

$\lambda=3, \lambda=-1$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the general solution of the original system, using the eigenvalues and eigenvectors determined in part 2.
parts.markscheme:
Eigenvectors:
For $\lambda=3$:
$\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow p=q$ <marks>M1</marks>
Eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

For $\lambda=-1$:
$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow p=-q$ <marks>A1</marks>
Eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

General solution for the homogeneous system:
$\begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

General solution for the original system:
$\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=5, y(0)=2$, find the particular solution and determine the limit of $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
At $t=0$:
$x(0) = A + B + 3 = 5 \Rightarrow A + B = 2$
$y(0) = A - B + 2 = 2 \Rightarrow A - B = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

Solving gives $A=1, B=1$ <marks>A1</marks>

Particular solution:
$\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

Limit as $t \rightarrow \infty$:
The dominant term is $e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
As $t \rightarrow \infty$, $x \approx e^{3t}$ and $y \approx e^{3t}$
$\frac{y}{x} \rightarrow 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A pharmacologist is modelling the concentration of a medication in a patient's bloodstream following an intramuscular injection. The variable $x$ represents the concentration of the medication in milligrams per litre ($\text{mg L}^{-1}$) at time $t$ hours after the injection.

The initial concentration in the bloodstream is $0 \, \text{mg L}^{-1}$ and the rate of increase of the concentration is initially $5 \, \text{mg L}^{-1} \text{hr}^{-1}$.

parts.content:
Show that the system of coupled first order differential equations:
$$ \frac{dx}{dt} = y, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = -6x - 5y $$
can be written as the second order differential equation:
$$ \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 5\frac{dx}{dt} + 6x = 0 $$
parts.markscheme:
- Differentiate the first equation with respect to $t$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dy}{dt}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $-6x - 5y$ for $\frac{dy}{dt}$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -6x - 5y$ 
  Substitute $\frac{dx}{dt}$ for $y$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -6x - 5\frac{dx}{dt}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Rearrange to the required form:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 5\frac{dx}{dt} + 6x = 0$ <marks>AG</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of the system of coupled first order equations given in part 1.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the relevant matrix:
  $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>(A1)</marks>

- Set up the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$:
  $-\lambda(-5-\lambda) + 6 = 0$ OR $\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$:
  $(\lambda + 2)(\lambda + 3) = 0$
  $\lambda = -2, -3$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Hence find the exact solution for the concentration $x$ at time $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the general solution:
  $x = Ae^{-2t} + Be^{-3t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Differentiate to find $\frac{dx}{dt}$:
  $\frac{dx}{dt} = -2Ae^{-2t} - 3Be^{-3t}$

- Apply initial conditions ($x=0$ and $\frac{dx}{dt}=5$ at $t=0$):
  $0 = A + B$
  $5 = -2A - 3B$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $A$ and $B$:
  $A = 5, B = -5$

- State the exact solution:
  $x = 5e^{-2t} - 5e^{-3t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $x$ against $t$, labelling the coordinates of the point where the concentration is at its maximum.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch graph showing correct shape: starts at $(0,0)$, reaches a peak, and then approaches the $t$-axis as an asymptote. <marks>A1</marks>

- Find maximum point coordinates:
  $\frac{dx}{dt} = -10e^{-2t} + 15e^{-3t} = 0 \implies e^t = 1.5 \implies t = \ln(1.5) \approx 0.405$
  $x(\ln 1.5) = 5\left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^2 - 5\left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^3 = \frac{20}{27} \approx 0.741$
  Maximum at $(\ln 1.5, \frac{20}{27})$ or $(0.405, 0.741)$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content:
The medication is considered to be therapeutically effective only when the concentration in the bloodstream is at least $0.5 \, \text{mg L}^{-1}$.

Calculate the total duration of time for which the medication is effective.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the condition $x = 0.5$:
  $5e^{-2t} - 5e^{-3t} = 0.5$ OR $e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} = 0.1$ <marks>(M1)</marks>

- Find the intersection points (using GDC or algebraic solver):
  $t_1 \approx 0.1288...$ and $t_2 \approx 0.8911...$ <marks>(A1)</marks>

- Calculate the difference:
  $0.8911... - 0.1288... = 0.7623...$
  Duration = $0.762$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Suggest one limitation of using this model to predict the drug concentration over a long period of time.
parts.markscheme:
- State a valid limitation with reference to the context. <marks>R1</marks>

  Examples:
  - The model assumes a constant rate of elimination which may change as the patient's metabolism adjusts.
  - Biological factors such as kidney or liver function are not constant over long periods.
  - Physical activity could change the rate of absorption from the muscle into the blood.
  - The model is a simplification and may not account for the drug's interaction with other substances in the body.

1 mark total

questions.specification:
A model for the spread of a rumor involves susceptible ( $x$ ) and informed ( $y$ ) individuals:

$$
\frac{d x}{d t}=-0.4 x y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=0.4 x y-0.1 y
$$

where $x$ and $y$ are in thousands and $t$ is in days.

parts.content: Show that the equilibrium set includes the line $y=0$ (for $x\ge 0$).
parts.markscheme:
- Set $\frac{d x}{d t}=0, \frac{d y}{d t}=0$: $-0.4 x y=0$ and $y(0.4 x-0.1)=0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- From first equation: $x=0$ or $y=0$

- From second equation: $y=0$ or $x=0.25$

- The intersection of these conditions includes $y=0$ (for any $x$) 

- Equilibria include $(x, 0)$ for $x \geq 0$ (a line along the $x$-axis) <marks>A1 A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: For the equilibrium point $(0,0)$, find the Jacobian matrix and its eigenvalues, and classify the stability.
parts.markscheme:
- Jacobian matrix: $J = \begin{pmatrix} -0.4 y & -0.4 x \\ 0.4 y & 0.4 x-0.1\end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- At $(0,0)$: $J = \begin{pmatrix}0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.1\end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Eigenvalues: $\lambda=0, -0.1$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Since one eigenvalue is $0$, linearization is inconclusive; note $(0,0)$ lies on the non-isolated equilibrium line $y=0$ <marks>R1</marks>

- Using $\frac{dy}{dt}=y(0.4x-0.1)$: for $0<x<0.25$ and $y>0$, $\frac{dy}{dt}<0$ so solutions are attracted towards $y=0$; for $x>0.25$ and $y>0$, $\frac{dy}{dt}>0$ so solutions move away from $y=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=8, y(0)=1$, use Euler's method with a step size of 0.1 days to estimate the populations after 0.3 days.
parts.markscheme:
- Euler's method: $x_{n+1}=x_{n}-0.1(0.4 x_{n} y_{n}), \quad y_{n+1}=y_{n}+0.1(0.4 x_{n} y_{n}- 0.1 y_{n})$ <marks>M1</marks>

- At $t=0$: $x_{0}=8, y_{0}=1$

- Step 1: $x_{1}=7.68, \quad y_{1}=1.31$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Step 2: $x_{2} \approx 7.278, \quad y_{2} \approx 1.699$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Step 3: $x_{3} \approx 6.78$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $y_{3} \approx 2.18$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Verify that $(0,0)$ is the only equilibrium point and find the eigenvalues of the system's coefficient matrix.
parts.markscheme:
- Show that for $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$ and $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$: 
  $x + 3y = 0 \implies x = -3y$ <marks>M1</marks>
  Substitute into second equation: $4(-3y) + 2y = 0 \implies -10y = 0 \implies y = 0, x = 0$. Hence $(0,0)$ is the only equilibrium point. <marks>A1</marks>

- Characteristic equation: $\det\left(\begin{pmatrix} 1-\lambda & 3 \\ 4 & 2-\lambda \end{pmatrix}\right) = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 12 = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
  $\lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 10 = 0 \implies (\lambda - 5)(\lambda + 2) = 0$

- Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 5, \lambda_2 = -2$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Determine the equations of the invariant lines that define the trajectories passing directly through the origin.
parts.markscheme:
- For $\lambda = 5$: $\begin{pmatrix} -4 & 3 \\ 4 & -3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \implies -4x + 3y = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
  Line equation: $y = \frac{4}{3}x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- For $\lambda = -2$: $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \implies 3x + 3y = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
  Line equation: $y = -x$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Express the general solution for $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ in terms of constants $C_1$ and $C_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- General solution in vector form: $\begin{pmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \end{pmatrix} = C_1 e^{5t} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} + C_2 e^{-2t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Separated equations: 
  $x(t) = 3C_1 e^{5t} + C_2 e^{-2t}$
  $y(t) = 4C_1 e^{5t} - C_2 e^{-2t}$
  <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Classify the equilibrium point at $(0,0)$ and determine its stability, providing a clear justification based on the eigenvalues.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification of eigenvalues: one positive ($+5$) and one negative ($-2$) <marks>R1</marks>
- Classification: Saddle point <marks>A1</marks>
- Stability: Unstable (because there is a positive eigenvalue/trajectories diverge along the $y = \frac{4}{3}x$ line) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Show that the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right)$ has only one eigenvalue, and find it along with an associated eigenvector.
parts.markscheme: Characteristic equation: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}2-\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)-(-1)(1)=\lambda^{2}- 3 \lambda+3=0 \quad$ (M1)A1. Discriminant: $9-12=-3 \Longrightarrow \lambda=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}$. No real roots, but check determinant: $(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)+1=\lambda^{2}-3 \lambda+3$. Try $\lambda=1.5$ : $(2-1.5)(1-1.5)+1=0.5 \cdot(-0.5)+1=0 \quad$ (M1)A1. Eigenvalue: $\lambda=1.5$. Eigenvector: $\left(\begin{array}{cc}0.5 & 1 \\ -1 & -0.5\end{array}\right)\binom{p}{q}=0 \Longrightarrow 0.5 p+q=0 \Longrightarrow q=-0.5 p$. Eigenvector: $\binom{2}{-1}$ (M1)A1. Note: M1 for determinant, A1 for equation, M1 for solving, A1 for eigenvalue, M1A1 for eigenvector. [6 marks]

parts.content: Verify that $\binom{x}{y}=\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}$ is a solution to the system.
parts.markscheme: Compute: $\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right)\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{2 \cdot 1+1 \cdot(-1)}{-1 \cdot 1+1 \cdot(-1)} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1}{-2} e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1)A1. Derivative: $\frac{d}{d t}\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1}{-1} 1.5 e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1)A1. Mismatch indicates recheck; correct solution needs adjustment. Try $\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}$ : Adjust coefficients later. Note: Award M1A1 per verification step, but solution needs general form check. [4 marks]

parts.content: Find a second independent solution of the form $\binom{t}{-t+k} e^{1.5 t}$, and verify it.
parts.markscheme:
Assume $\binom{x}{y}=\binom{t}{-t+k} e^{1.5 t}$. Derivative: $\frac{d}{d t}\binom{t}{-t+k} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1+1.5 t}{-1+1.5(-t+k)} e^{1.5 t}$ System: $\frac{d x}{d t}=(1+1.5 t) e^{1.5 t}=2 t e^{1.5 t}+(-t+k) e^{1.5 t}$. Simplify: $k=1$. Verify
second equation: Matches. Solution: $\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t}$ (M1)A1A1A1. Note: M1 for form, M1 for derivative, A1 per verification. [5 marks]

parts.content: Given initial conditions $x(0)=5, y(0)=0$, find the particular solution.
parts.markscheme:
General solution: $\binom{x}{y}=A\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}+B\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1). At $t=0$ : $x=A=5, y=-A+B=0 \Longrightarrow B=5$. Solution: $\binom{x}{y}=5\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}+ 5\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t}$ A1A1. Note: M1 for setup, A1 per constant. [3 marks]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_08_18_92f94e14538350f9cb64g-14.jpg?height=758&width=747&top_left_y=759&top_left_x=439)

A1 for axes, A1 for equilibrium and eigenvector, A1 for trajectories, A1 for arrows. [4 marks]

parts.content: Sketch the phase portrait for $-3 \leq x \leq 3,-3 \leq y \leq 3$, showing the equilibrium point, an eigenvector direction, and at least two trajectories with arrows.
parts.markscheme:
Characteristic equation: $\left|\begin{array}{cc}2-\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1-\lambda\end{array}\right|=(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)-(-1)(1)=\lambda^{2}- 3 \lambda+3=0 \quad$ (M1)A1. Discriminant: $9-12=-3 \Longrightarrow \lambda=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}$. No real roots, but check determinant: $(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)+1=\lambda^{2}-3 \lambda+3$. Try $\lambda=1.5$ : $(2-1.5)(1-1.5)+1=0.5 \cdot(-0.5)+1=0 \quad$ (M1)A1. Eigenvalue: $\lambda=1.5$. Eigenvector: $\left(\begin{array}{cc}0.5 & 1 \\ -1 & -0.5\end{array}\right)\binom{p}{q}=0 \Longrightarrow 0.5 p+q=0 \Longrightarrow q=-0.5 p$. Eigenvector: $\binom{2}{-1}$ (M1)A1. Note: M1 for determinant, A1 for equation, M1 for solving, A1 for eigenvalue, M1A1 for eigenvector. [6 marks]
(b) Compute: $\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right)\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{2 \cdot 1+1 \cdot(-1)}{-1 \cdot 1+1 \cdot(-1)} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1}{-2} e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1)A1. Derivative: $\frac{d}{d t}\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1}{-1} 1.5 e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1)A1. Mismatch indicates recheck; correct solution needs adjustment. Try $\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}$ : Adjust coefficients later. Note: Award M1A1 per verification step, but solution needs general form check. [4 marks]
(c) Assume $\binom{x}{y}=\binom{t}{-t+k} e^{1.5 t}$. Derivative: $\frac{d}{d t}\binom{t}{-t+k} e^{1.5 t}=\binom{1+1.5 t}{-1+1.5(-t+k)} e^{1.5 t}$ System: $\frac{d x}{d t}=(1+1.5 t) e^{1.5 t}=2 t e^{1.5 t}+(-t+k) e^{1.5 t}$. Simplify: $k=1$. Verify
second equation: Matches. Solution: $\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t}$ (M1)A1A1A1. Note: M1 for form, M1 for derivative, A1 per verification. [5 marks]
(d) General solution: $\binom{x}{y}=A\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}+B\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t} \quad$ (M1). At $t=0$ : $x=A=5, y=-A+B=0 \Longrightarrow B=5$. Solution: $\binom{x}{y}=5\binom{1}{-1} e^{1.5 t}+ 5\binom{t}{-t+1} e^{1.5 t}$ A1A1. Note: M1 for setup, A1 per constant. [3 marks]

questions.specification:
A model for the spread of a disease in a population is given by the following system of differential equations:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = -0.3xy, \quad \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 0.3xy - 0.2y$$

where $x$ represents the number of susceptible individuals (in thousands), $y$ represents the number of infected individuals (in thousands), and $t$ is the time in weeks.
